tags.description: Cathedral-like cave with sunlight beams creating light effects through schools of bait fish. Shallow cave mouth at 4.5-15m, bottom at 18m. Famous for swirling baitfish patterns and nudibranchs. Best at midday for light effects.

Shallow coral reef with vibrant hard and soft corals, gorgonian fans. Slopes from 2.4m to 15m. Ideal for longer bottom time with sea turtles, nudibranchs, tropical reef fish. Excellent year-round visibility.

Classic Vavau wall dive with a gradual drop-off to 40m. Features healthy coral gardens, schools of trevally and barracuda, occasional manta rays during summer months. Good visibility year-round (20-30m). Perfect for intermediate divers exploring Tonga's wall diving.

Shallow wreck site in Vavau group, depths 20-25m. Hull structure provides shelter for wrasse, snapper, and reef fish. Light penetration through hull allows exploration of enclosed spaces. Coral encrustation and soft corals adorn the structure. Suitable for intermediate divers with wreck experience.

Wall dive in whale-frequented areas. Likely outer reef wall with excellent visibility 25-40m. Humpback whale sightings July-November (snorkel-based, no scuba bubbles). Healthy corals, rays, sharks, reef fish.

Remote volcanic island dive site in Tonga, 70km northwest of Vavau. Strong currents common, require Advanced skills. Pristine coral gardens and exceptional pelagic action with jacks, tuna, sharks. Limited accessibility due to distance and weather conditions. Best suited for experienced liveaboard divers.

Underwater cave entrance accessible by snorkeling (1-4m) or scuba (12-15m). Famous for ethereal blue glow and dramatic air compression creating fog. Strong swimming required. Wall dive with swim-throughs to 12m. Named after William Mariner, 1806 survivor. Best west-facing in mid-afternoon.

Premier advanced diving destination with Cathedral Cave - one of world's largest underwater sea caves (100m long, 50m wide). Entrance at 28m, dramatic hallway with light filtering through roof holes creating supernatural feeling. Surrounding healthy coral gardens with cardinal fish, butterfly fish, sweetlips. Depths 28-70m depending on specific site. Visibility 20-40m.

tags.name: Takuo
tags.description: Historical shipwreck in southern Tonga waters, hull visible at surface. Depths 18-20m provide good light penetration for exploring the deteriorated structure. Coral encrustation and macro life abundant. Moderate currents typical. Suitable intermediate wreck site.

tags.name: Marmer
tags.description: Also known as Lady Lata. Historical ship wreck at very shallow 2-2.4m depth. OSM records indicate submerged but marked as dangerous. Likely minimal dive activity due to extreme shallowness and possible silt.

tags.name: Ata
tags.description: Very remote southern Tonga island with shallow reef dive (12-15m). Pristine conditions with strong current require caution despite beginner depth. Excellent visibility and healthy hard corals. Pelagic fish common in current. Accessible only by liveaboard.

tags.name: Hakau Mounu
tags.description: Mounu Island atoll system reef with famous manta ray cleaning station. Seasonal manta encounters July-November. Reef slopes from 5m to 20m with coral gardens and reef fish. Intermediate-level exploration of pristine Tonga waters.

tags.name: Hakau Ualanga 'Uta
tags.description: Hakau group fringing reef with Tongan name meaning 'inner lagoon reef.' Shallow plateau reef 8-18m, excellent for intermediate divers. Healthy coral growth and reef fish schools. Lower current typical for inner lagoon location.

tags.name: Hakau Tapu
tags.description: Hakau Tapu (sacred reef) outer fringing reef with excellent visibility 25-35m. Coral slopes and boulders from 3m down to 20m. Mixed coral and soft coral gardens. Good intermediate dive with moderate currents typical of outer reef location.
